data.title: Kingdom Holdings Expects to Swing to 2024 Net Loss From CNY162.8M Profit in 2023
FTSE Straits Times Index Closes 0.4% Higher at 3830.42
FTSE 100 Index Hits New High After AstraZeneca, Anglo American Reports — Market Talk
data.description: The FTSE 100 index has hit a new intra-day record, boosted by AstraZeneca and Anglo American after both companies issued reports, and on expectations that the Bank of England will cut interest rates again later Thursday. The index hit a high of 8,721, with AstraZeneca shares up 4.8% and Anglo American shares trading 4.3% higher. Earlier AstraZeneca said that it expects further sales and earnings growth this year after beating market forecasts in the latest quarter, while Anglo American reported market-beating production figures for the same period. The BoE is expected to lower interest rates by 25 basis points from the current 4.75%, which was lowered by the same amount in November. (ian.walker@wsj.com)

data.title: Belite Bio Signs Securities Purchase Deal for its American Depositary Shares, Warrants
data.description:
Belite Bio said late Wednesday it signed a securities purchase deal with a new healthcare investor to buy and sell 258,309 of its American depositary shares and warrants to buy equal amount of American depositary shares at $58.07 per ADS in a registered direct offering for gross proceeds of roughly $15 million.

The biopharmaceutical company intends to close the offering around Friday, and plans to use the net proceeds for working capital and general corporate purposes, it added.

Belite Bio's stock was down more than 1% in recent Thursday premarket activity.

data.title: Prompt prices fall on projected jump in German wind power supply
data.description:
European spot power prices fell on Thursday as LSEG data projected that German wind power generation would more than treble for the day ahead.

LSEG analysis also said that lower thermal capacity availability was bullish but noted that demand is expected to drop in the region day on day.

French baseload for the day ahead (TRFRBD1) lost 10.8% to 130 euros ($134.75) per megawatt hour (MWh) by 0905 GMT.

The German equivalent was down nearly 23% at 117.8 euros. (TRDEBD1)

Germany was forecast to produce 31.2 gigawatts (GW) of wind power on Friday, more than three times the 9.4 GW expected on Thursday. France, meanwhile, was forecast to generate 1.4 GW more wind power at 8.4 GW, the data showed.

French nuclear availability rose by two percentage points to 83% of installed capacity.

On the demand side, power usage in Germany was expected to drop by 500 MW day on day to 63.1 GW. French demand was forecast to fall by 1.3 GW to 67.7 GW.

Along the curve, German year-ahead baseload (TRDEBYZ6) was up 0.3% at 98.7 euros/MWh while the French year-ahead position (TRFRBYZ6) was untraded after settling at 69.3 euros/MWh.

The European carbon market's benchmark 2025 contract (CFI2Zc1) added 0.9% to 81.84 euros a metric ton.

European data centres will expand capacity by 22% this year, industry analysts told a conference in Amsterdam. Data hubs' power demand is already causing some grid constraints.

Power trading on the Epex Spot bourse in January increased by 11.5% year on year to 82.6 TWh.

Europe’s battery storage capacity is expected to show a fivefold increase by 2030, say developers and traders, as the cost of new projects falls.

Sweden's Vattenfall said it has sold 52% of Nordic power production for 2025 at an average 48 euros/MWh in forward hedges, having achieved 42 euros last year in spot sales and hedges.

($1 = 0.9648 euros)

data.title: Itochu Considering Investment in 7-Eleven Owner — Update
data.description:
By Kosaku Narioka

Japanese trading house Itochu is considering investing in the owner of 7-Eleven, emerging as the latest player in an international takeover battle over one of the world's biggest convenience-store operators.

Itochu Chief Financial Officer Tsuyoshi Hachimura said Thursday that the company is considering investing in Seven & i Holdings. An Itochu spokeswoman confirmed the comments he made at an earnings briefing earlier in the day.

The 7-Eleven owner in September rejected an initial $39 billion buyout bid from Canada's Alimentation Couche-Tard, saying the proposal underestimated the company's value. Couche-Tard later raised its offer to about $47 billion.

In November, Seven & i said it had received a buyout proposal from Junro Ito, a top Seven & i executive and son of the founder of the Japanese retail giant, but didn't provide details.

Private-equity firm KKR was considering joining the Seven & i founding family to take the company private, though talks were in the very initial stages, The Wall Street Journal reported in January.

Itochu hasn't decided whether it would invest in Seven & i or how, the spokeswoman said Thursday.

Seven & i Chief Financial Officer Yoshimichi Maruyama said last month that the company was considering options, including Couche-Tard's bid, Ito's proposal and a standalone growth strategy.

Seven & i has said it will bring in strategic partners for its business of supermarkets, specialty stores and other operations and spin off and list the holding company.

The Japanese company has said it will continue to consider strategic initiatives to unlock its North American convenience-store business's potential value as well as optimal capital relations with its banking unit. The retail giant has said it will accelerate expansion to new regions to seek growth in markets worldwide.

Write to Kosaku Narioka at kosaku.narioka@wsj.com

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0923 GMT - Babcock International's decision to raise its full-year guidance comes amid a strong performance of its nuclear and marine units, which grew 22% and 6% respectively, Jefferies analysts Chloe Lemarie and Ben Brown say. The nuclear division in particular is now expected to grow by double digits in 2025, with the civil-nuclear, submarine-support and infrastructure units performing better than expected, they write. The U.K. defense company remains one of Jefferies's preferred names and is trading close to its record discount to European defense peers, at 44%, the analysts note. Shares are down 0.2% at 5.43 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0922 GMT - The FTSE 100 index has hit a new intra-day record, boosted by AstraZeneca and Anglo American after both companies issued reports, and on expectations that the Bank of England will cut interest rates again later Thursday. The index hit a high of 8,721, with AstraZeneca shares up 4.8% and Anglo American shares trading 4.3% higher. Earlier AstraZeneca said that it expects further sales and earnings growth this year after beating market forecasts in the latest quarter, while Anglo American reported market-beating production figures for the same period. The BoE is expected to lower interest rates by 25 basis points from the current 4.75%, which was lowered by the same amount in November. (ian.walker@wsj.com)

0918 GMT - Babcock International should continue to benefit from healthy revenue growth opportunities soundly financed by its balance sheet, Shore Capital analysts Robin Speakman and Jamie Murray say. Global defense spending continues to rise and the trend to invest in nuclear, including decommissioning, supports Babcock's business, they write. The analysts expect the U.K. defense company to continue to post broadly positive results in 2026 and 2027, but caution that the group is yet to issue guidance on the impact that the raise to national insurance contributions announced by the U.K. government in its budget last October will have over the business. Shares are down 0.2% at 5.43 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0909 GMT - Carlsberg reported a solid performance in 2024, with sales and EBIT broadly in line with expectations and delivering earnings ahead of market estimates, Citi analysts say. The Danish brewer posted surprising adjusted EPS for the year of 54.9 Danish kronor, around 5% ahead of consensus, they say in a note. Looking ahead, Carlsberg expects 2025 organic EBIT growth between 1% to 5% after including the 200bps to 300bps impact from the loss of San Miguel. "Overall, this is in line with our expectations and consensus estimates of around 3% but is slightly better than some were fearing ahead of the print," they say. EPS estimates in 2025 could be upgraded by a low single-digit percentage growth, the analysts add. Shares are up 2.1% at 858 Danish kronor. (michael.susin@wsj.com)

0909 GMT - China's retail data during Lunar New Year holiday is not enough to support a positive outlook, HSBC analysts Lina Yan and Yimin Wang in a research note. Retail sales grew 5% but the analysts don't read too much into the that as it could be merely a release of spending power after a weak 2024. Spending patterns matter more and they flag some trends that could persist in 2025. These include cost-conscious spending, more demand for certain products due to government stimulus, young consumers leading consumption. Rather than hoping for a turnaround in the consumption cycle, their top picks for 2025 include Giant Biogene for product strength; Anta for resilient earnings growth; Yum China for shareholder returns; and CR Beverage for value relative to profit growth. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0908 GMT - Malaysia's benchmark Kuala Lumpur Composite Index ended 0.7% higher at 1585.17. Market sentiment stayed positive as concerns of a full-scale trade war between the U.S. and China eased, alongside hopes for potential concession deals to delay or reduce tariffs, similarly to Mexico and Canada, TA Securities analyst Stephen Soo says in a note. Soo pegs the KLCI's support at 1550 and resistance at 1605. Among gainers, Petronas Gas was 2.5% higher and Gamuda Bhd. added 2.1%. Meanwhile, IOI Corp. was 1.1% lower and Mr. D.I.Y. Group lost 0.6%. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0905 GMT - Crude futures find some support in early trade after Saudi Arabia lifted prices for March loadings of all crude grades it exports amid surging premiums for Middle Eastern oil. State-owned oil giant Aramco's flagship Arab Light crude to Asia, its main market, was raised by $2.40 a barrel to $3.90 a barrel over the Oman/Dubai benchmark--the largest monthly increase since August 2022. "This ties in with the strength that we have seen in the Middle East physical market since the start of the year," ING analysts say. Brent crude is up 0.1% at $74.71 a barrel, while WTI rises 0.3% to $71.22 a barrel. The benchmarks settled 2% lower in the previous trading session due to a larger-than-expected build in U.S. crude stockpiles and fears that a trade war between the U.S. and China could hurt global growth. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0906 GMT - Anglo American reported a decent fourth-quarter production update, Barclays analysts write. Its key iron ore, copper and platinum group metals divisions were broadly ahead of consensus. However, sales volumes were mixed while realized prices were weaker than expected, they write. Anglo's medium-term copper guidance is unchanged but new 2027 guidance of 760,000 to 820,000 tons is lower than expected, they write. Shares trade up 4.4% at 2435.50 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0904 GMT - Orsted's 2025 Ebitda guidance of 25 billion to 28 billion Danish kroner is in line with consensus at 27.1 billion kroner, RBC Capital Markets analysts Alexander Wheeler and Ziyad Jasimuddin write. Gross investment guidance of 50 billion to 54 billion is also in line with the 52.5 billion expected by RBC. The company yesterday announced it was dropping its 2030 EBITDA and installed capacity targets, while reducing its gross investments to 2030 by around 25%. "We think that the decision to reduce the investment targets will be more of a focus on the earnings call as the market looks to Orsted's balance sheet and what impact this may have." Shares rise 5.5% to 296.30 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0848 GMT - Warpaint London's pretax profit of 24 million pounds being in line with consensus suggests margins have continued to improve from the 23% reported in 2023, RBC Capital Markets analysts Tania Maciver and Ben Pfannes-Varrow say in a note. The cosmetics company's strong performance into January, with year-on-year revenue up 15%, and continued margin improvement further endorses its strategy, the analysts say. They forecast 2025 revenue of 132 million pounds, representing on-year growth of 24%. "While the top line appears to have been impacted by continuing consumer spending headwinds, Warpaint's profit before tax... is in line with consensus and our forecasts," the analysts say. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0843 GMT - Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index closed 1.4% higher at 20891.62, tracking U.S. futures. With some easing of anxiety about U.S. tariffs, market attention is redirected to U.S. economic data releases this week, which will provide more clues about the health of the economy and the Fed's next move, ANZ Research writes in a note. BYD jumped 11.5% after the company said it will host a press conference next week about its smart-car strategy. Sunny Optical Technology gained 9.9% and SMIC was 7.2% higher. Among decliners, WH Group lost 1.6%, Baidu fell 1.3% and Galaxy Entertainment was 1.0% lower. The Hang Seng Tech Index rose 2.6% to end at 5059.38, a 2025 high. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0839 GMT - Compass Group has a high-quality earnings stream and growth prospects despite an uncertain environment, Shore Capital analyst Greg Johnson writes in a note. The London-listed catering contractor's first-quarter organic sales growth was slightly above forecast and ahead of full-year assumptions, Johnson says. Pricing and volumes will likely ease through the year, with like-for-like volumes a potential positive driver to forecasts, he adds. Shares are down 1.9% at 2,737 pence.(najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: Babcock International Leapfrogs Thanks to Strength of Nuclear — Market Talk
data.description: Babcock International's decision to raise its full-year guidance comes amid a strong performance of its nuclear and marine units, which grew 22% and 6% respectively, Jefferies analysts Chloe Lemarie and Ben Brown say. The nuclear division in particular is now expected to grow by double digits in 2025, with the civil-nuclear, submarine-support and infrastructure units performing better than expected, they write. The U.K. defense company remains one of Jefferies's preferred names and is trading close to its record discount to European defense peers, at 44%, the analysts note. Shares are down 0.2% at 5.43 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: Court Convicts Former Employees of Guangzhou Tinci Materials Technology Unit for Trade Secret Violation
data.description:
Jiangxi Province's Lianxi District Court ruled Guangzhou Tinci Materials Technology subsidiary Jiujiang Tinci's former employees, Li Sheng and Zheng Feiling, were guilty of infringing on trade secrets, according to a Thursday filing with the Shenzhen Stock Exchange.

Li and Zheng were convicted of disclosing proprietary lithium battery electrolyte production technologies to Zhejiang-based companies.

Li was sentenced to four years and four months with a 4.5 million yuan fine, while Zheng received three and four months with a 1.5 million yuan fine.

data.title: Jefferies Adjusts Shimadzu's Price Target to 5,500 Yen From 5,800 Yen, Keeps at Buy
data.description: Shimadzu has an average rating of outperform and price targets ranging from 3,900 yen to 6,100 yen, according to analysts polled by Capital IQ.

data.title: COMMENT-Options flag extent of U.S. NFP risk to Forex market
data.description:
Overnight options now expire at 10:00 a.m. New York/15.00 GMT on Friday, and therefore include January's U.S. jobs data, with the increase in related premiums showing the expected FX market reaction.

FX volatility is a key, yet unknown, parameter of an FX option premium, so dealers use implied volatility - their best estimate. Any increase in implied volatility when its related expiry date includes a potentially market-moving event will therefore show how much additional FX realised volatility that event is expected to generate.

Since including the U.S. jobs data, overnight expiry EUR/USD implied volatility has increased from 13.0 to 15.0. The premium/break-even for a simple vanilla straddle is up from 56 USD pips to 65 USD pips in either direction.

Overnight USD/JPY implied volatility has increased from 14.0 to 17.5 - a premium/break-even of 89 JPY pips to 111 JPY pips in either direction.

GBP/USD overnight options failed to add any premium for Thursday's Bank of England policy decision, but their implied volatility has increased from 14.0 to 17.0 for the U.S. jobs data, which is a premium/break-even of 73 USD pips to 88 USD pips in either direction.

AUD/USD overnight expiry implied volatility is up from 9.5 to 12.5, or 25 USD pips to 33 USD pips in either direction.

For context, these implied volatility increases and higher levels (with the exception of USD/JPY) are less than those posted before the January 10 release of December's U.S. jobs data, suggesting the FX market isn't expecting a significant FX reaction.

For more click on

data.title: Lotte Himart Widens Loss in Q4 2024
data.description:
Lotte Himart widened its net loss to 316.2 billion won in the fourth quarter from 46.5 billion won a year earlier.

Sales of the electronic goods retailer declined 3.8% year over year to 556.4 billion won from 578.5 billion won, according to a Wednesday Korea Exchange filing.

Shares of Lotte Himart fell nearly 1% in recent trade on Thursday.

data.title: Nippon Steel Logs 18% Lower Attributable Profit for Nine Months to December 2024
data.description:
Nippon Steel recorded a profit attributable to owners of the parent of 362.1 billion yen for the nine months ended Dec. 31, 2024, 17.9% lower than 440.9 billion yen logged for the same period a year ago.

The company's earnings per share for the reporting period decreased to 346.52 yen from 423.94 yen per share a year ago, according to a Thursday filing with the Tokyo Exchange.

Revenue of the steel company came in at 6.552 trillion yen, 1.3% lower than 6.642 trillion yen a year earlier.

It forecasted an attributable profit of 310 billion yen, a basic EPS of 311 yen, a revenue of 8.600 trillion yen, and a final dividend of 80 yen for the fiscal year ending March. 31.

data.title: Ayvens falls after Q4 operating income miss
data.description:
** Shares in French car leasing company Ayvens fall 4%, as it publishes Q4 operating income below market expectations

** Its Q4 gross operating income reaches 712.9 million euros ($738.71 million), below the 740.1 million euros expected in a company-compiled consensus, ING says

** "Fleet growth remains an issue as the total fleet shrank by 3.6% to 3.298 million vehicles," ING analysts say, adding it the figure was 1.3% below consensus

** Its results from used car sales also shrinks 59.8% YoY at 37.7 million euros, below the 65.2% expected in the consensus, the broker adds

** Shares in Ayvens are on track for their worst day since November 12, if losses hold

($1 = 0.9651 euros)

data.title: Babcock International to Benefit From Healthy Revenue Growth Opportunities — Market Talk
data.description: Babcock International should continue to benefit from healthy revenue growth opportunities soundly financed by its balance sheet, Shore Capital analysts Robin Speakman and Jamie Murray say. Global defense spending continues to rise and the trend to invest in nuclear, including decommissioning, supports Babcock's business, they write. The analysts expect the U.K. defense company to continue to post broadly positive results in 2026 and 2027, but caution that the group is yet to issue guidance on the impact that the raise to national insurance contributions announced by the U.K. government in its budget last October will have over the business. Shares are down 0.2% at 5.43 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: 'The U.K. is not a basket case.' Keep calm and buy British bonds, this Dutch advisor suggests.
data.description:
By Steve Goldstein

British government bonds are attractive, a Dutch advisor is saying, citing their positive inflation-adjusted yield and relative value compared to other European government bonds.

In a presentation, AFS Group research director Arne Petimezas argued the U.K. is not the basket case that many investors make it out to be. While the country's economic growth has been slow, it is in line with its peers, and inflation is under control.

He also points out that the U.K.'s debt-to-GDP ratio is not as high as some of its European counterparts, such as Italy and Greece, and its primary fiscal balance to GDP is in the middle of the pack. Despite this, these countries enjoy low yields, making gilts a more attractive investment opportunity.

One of the key drivers of AFS Group's positive outlook on gilts is the relative value they offer compared to other European government bonds. As Petimezas notes, the U.K.'s inflation rate is in line with that of its European peers, yet gilt BX:TMBMKGB-10Y yields are higher, providing a positive inflation-adjusted yield.

In contrast, the yields on German bunds BX:TMBMKDE-10Yare extremely low, with Petimezas noting that they have a negative yield adjusted for inflation. "They don't pay s**t," he said. This makes gilts an attractive alternative for investors seeking higher returns.

The U.K.'s economy is relatively insulated from trade tensions, as President Donald Trump has hinted that Britain may avoid tariffs.

Petimezas says this pitch is aimed at clients who buy bonds in British pounds, as the hedged yield for euro clients is not attractive.

-Steve Goldstein

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Robinsons Land Repurchases More Shares
data.description:
Robinsons Land repurchased 780,000 shares at between 12.26 Philippine pesos and 12.68 pesos each on Wednesday, according to a Thursday Philippine bourse disclosure.

The property developer has so far spent 6.03 billion pesos out of the 9 billion pesos allocated under its buyback program.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0837 GMT - WeChat's e-commerce system could gain stronger awareness among consumers in 2025 through a broader adoption of its gifting function, Citi analysts say in a research note. The Tencent-owned popular messaging app in December rolled out a gifting function, which has successfully expanded the consumption scenarios within the app's ecosystem during the Lunar New Year, the analysts note. Citi predicts the function to be more widely used, thanks to the app's "social graph" alongside a growing number of participating merchants. Citi maintains a buy rating on Tencent, with its target price unchanged at HK$563.00. Shares of Tencent last ended at HK$420.40. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0803 GMT - U.S. tariffs will likely have a minimal impact on software companies, while hardware companies will face a bigger hit, Janus Henderson Investors says in a commentary. Software isn't reliant on physical asset production and has a geographical distributed workforce, whereas hardware is often manufactured abroad and relies on components such as semiconductor chips and wafers. The components are mainly produced in Asia-Pacific and then shipped to Mexico for assembly to take advantage of lower labor costs, the asset manager adds. "The impact of the recently announced tariffs would therefore be most pronounced in the assembly phase." Janus Henderson expects the impact on large end-market manufacturers like Dell and HP to be more acute than their suppliers. (farah.elias@wsj.com)

0506 GMT - The magnitude of price cuts in China's electric-vehicle industry this year should be much less than last year, Jefferies analysts write in a note. Since inventories at BYD dealers are much lower and the profitability of new car sales is much better, the analysts reckon BYD is unlikely to kick off an aggressive price war like last year. Among the top Chinese EV makers, Xiaomi, Huawei-backed Harmony Intelligent Mobility Alliance and XPeng are outperformers by new order intakes, they note. The introduction of Tesla's full-self driving features in China this year could serve as a good example for its Chinese rivals on how carmakers can commercialize their advanced assisted driving technologies, they add. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0401 GMT - Chinese startup DeepSeek's reasoning model could significantly cut AI training and inference costs, help AI technology expand and diversify AI applications, HSBC Global Research analysts write in a note. The adoption of DeepSeek-R1 could help software companies improve research and development efficiency, save costs and boost earnings growth in the medium term, they add. DeepSeek's reportedly cheap yet powerful model could help boost the monetization of AI applications, especially in the Chinese market, they add. The brokerage expects AI agents to contribute significant revenues to software companies early in 2H. AI is also likely to be a revenue driver for leading software players amid China's soft macroeconomic conditions, it says. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0348 GMT - Investors should take a selective approach to the Malaysian technology sector, focusing on stocks with clear strategies, long-term vision, and earnings visibility through secured orders, says AmInvestment Bank Analyst Paul Yap Ee Xing in a note. Equipment makers offer the best risk-reward, supported by stable earnings forecasts, a recovering order book, and trends in supply chain relocation, he reckons. While high earnings expectations pose a risk, Yap still sees value in the sector, given low investor holdings and strong long-term growth potential. As AI and data center trades unwind, investors seeking to rebalance their portfolio may return, he adds. AmInvestment Bank maintains an overweight rating on Malaysia's technology sector and pegs Vitrox Corp. and VS Industry as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0345 GMT - SGH remains a buy for UBS analysts, who anticipate ongoing earnings momentum from the Australian engineering and construction group. They think that the stock's recent rerate to 20 times earnings on a one-year forward basis, compared with a 10-year average of 13X, has been underpinned by investors' greater appreciation of its proven earnings delivery and investment discipline. With operational discipline at the Boral business now under SGH's control and mining maintenance demand looking strong at its WesTrac unit, they forecast average annual earnings growth of 8% for the next three fiscal years. UBS raises its target price 24% to A$56.00. Shares are up 0.7% at A$49.32. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1651 GMT - Snap's 4Q beat and optimistic outlook were encouraging, say analysts at BofA Securities in a research note, but user growth remains limited in high value US/EU markets. The analysts also say the app transition rollout suggests mixed results, and that big EBITDA upside seems less likely given growing expenses. BofA says Snap's stock-based compensation expense exceeds the investment bank's free cash flow estimates through 2027. Snap slides 7%. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1613 GMT - Apple shares are under modest pressure amid reports that Chinese regulators are considering opening a formal investigation into App Store's fees and policies, even if revenue from the App Store in China is a manageable $5 billion, Wedbush analysts say in a research note. "So its less about revenue exposure for investors and more about building US/China tensions with US Big Tech in line for retaliatory shots across the bow," the analysts say, noting that China recently opened an antitrust probe into Alphabet's Google. "This is all a game of high stakes poker between Trump and China and Apple is clearly a chip on the table," the analysts add. Apple shares fall 1% to $230.39. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1333 GMT - ATS thinks that its worldwide scale can help jittery customers navigate U.S. tariffs. On the earnings call, executives say they have been discussing tariffs with customers who are cautious about the situation, but the company feels secure about its ability to support client supply chain complexities. ATS points to the company's global footprint as a big factor for stability--with a substantial presence in the U.S., Germany as well as Canada. "Conversations are more on the positive side, but clients are waiting to see how things will shake out," executives say. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1257 GMT - Goldman Sachs economists think the risks of tariff escalation remain very real. The bank's team now projects that the U.S. will enact a 10% global tariff on critical imports such as semiconductors and pharmaceuticals as its baseline forecast, as well as a tariff on cars from the EU. If its current expected scenario is implemented, the tariffs could erase most progress on inflation otherwise expected this year, leaving core PCE inflation at 2.6% over the 12 months through December, Goldman projects. If Canada and Mexico tariffs are implemented after all, inflation would instead increase, peaking just above 3%, Goldman writes. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1155 GMT - Uber says it expects gross bookings in the current quarter of $42 billion-$43.5 billion, with the top end of that range coming in line with Wall Street's consensus view, according to FactSet. The 1Q outlook assumes a currency headwind to total growth of about 5.5 percentage points, the ride-hailing company says. Shares fall 5.3% premarket, despite 4Q results otherwise coming in ahead of Street views. (mark.long@wsj.com)

1018 GMT - Google owner Alphabet reported weaker-than-expected growth at its cloud-computing business, AJ Bell's Dan Coatsworth writes in a research note. Google Cloud revenue grew to nearly $11.96 billion in the fourth quarter from $9.19 billion a year earlier, below a FactSet forecast of $12.16 billion. Weaker-than-expected growth in cloud computing suggests the AI frenzy isn't automatically translating into big bucks for infrastructure providers, Coatsworth says. Alphabet shares are down 7.4% premarket at $191.12. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1529 GMT - GSK has to prove its pipeline is packed with new blockbuster treatments to withstand investors' concerns over its vaccine business slowdown in China and the U.S., AJ Bell investment director Russ Mould says. Further worries over the likely appointment of vaccine-sceptic Robert F Kennedy Jr. as Secretary of Health and Human Services in the U.S. still linger, he says. The British pharma giant's posted a second consecutive drop in sales in the fourth quarter, driven by a 19% decline in sales of its flu shots and a 52% plunge for its respiratory treatment Arexvy. Shares are up 6.7% at 14.73 pounds.(helena.smolak@wsj.com)

1525 GMT - GSK's 2025 outlook is promising, fueled by the sales growth expectations for its specialty medicines, Quilter Cheviot healthcare analyst Sheena Berry says in a note. The British pharma giant's clinical results for its chronic-cough drug candidate and regulatory decisions on a potential comeback of its blood-disorder treatment Blenrep are crucial for the group's future trajectory, she says. Shares are up 6.7% at 14.73 pounds.(helena.smolak@wsj.com)

1257 GMT - Goldman Sachs economists think the risks of tariff escalation remain very real. The bank's team now projects that the U.S. will enact a 10% global tariff on critical imports such as semiconductors and pharmaceuticals as its baseline forecast, as well as a tariff on cars from the EU. If its current expected scenario is implemented, the tariffs could erase most progress on inflation otherwise expected this year, leaving core PCE inflation at 2.6% over the 12 months through December, Goldman projects. If Canada and Mexico tariffs are implemented after all, inflation would instead increase, peaking just above 3%, Goldman writes. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1135 GMT - With anxiety running high into Novo Nordisk's results, a decent topline beat for the quarter, despite a slight Wegovy miss, coupled with strong guidance for 2025 is good enough to get the story back on track, Barclays analysts write. Sales were 5% above company consensus with Ozempic in line and Wegovy 2% below. 2025 topline guidance of 16%-24% is above Barclays expectations. "We think this is a good enough print to get shares moving back in the right direction." Shares trade 3.1% higher at 611 Danish kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0835 GMT - The Bank of Japan should raise interest rates to at least above 1% if economic growth continues on greater flexibility in the job market and productivity improvements, says Masamichi Koike, deputy president and executive officer at Japanese megabank Sumitomo Mitsui Banking Corp. However, given that the country still hasn't completely overcome a deflationary mindset, "there may be a shock [to the economy] if the BOJ raises rates too rapidly," he says at an event in Tokyo. Meanwhile, Japan's corporate sector will likely witness more consolidations and bankruptcies this year as the economic recovery spurs healthy competition, he says. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0822 GMT - Max Financial Services' life-insurance unit seems well placed to achieve its FY 2025 guidance, Nomura analysts say in a research report. For the unit, management guides for 20% annual-premium-equivalent growth and high-single-digit growth for value of new business in FY 2025, the analysts note. Management appears reasonably confident that the unit can post a VNB margin of 23%-24% in FY 2025, the analysts say. With the effects of India's surrender value regulations for the insurance industry now mostly factored in, the company appears well on course to deliver on the guidance, the analysts add. Nomura upgrades the stock to buy from neutral and raises the target price to INR1,300.00 from INR1,250.00. Shares are 1.0% lower at INR1,107.60. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0810 GMT - Hannover Re's confirmation of its guidance for 2025 is positive in light of the Los Angeles wildfires, which have been a clear drag on the reinsurance sector year to date, analysts at JPMorgan say. The German reinsurer didn't provide an estimate on the impact of the California fires, but it did reaffirm its previous guidance for net profit of 2.4 billion euros this year. It also confirmed earnings for last year at 2.3 billion euros, in line with previous guidance and said it achieved premium growth of 7.6% at its January treaty renewals with pricing reduced 2.1% on a risk-adjusted basis. "Price reductions for Hannover Re take profitability back to 2023 levels which was a strong baseline in our view," JPM says. Shares rise 0.3%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0719 GMT - Societe Generale's better-than-expected results and guidance for 2025 that points to more cost controls should provide confidence that its 2026 targets are in reach, RBC Capital Markets' Anke Reingen and Matthew Russell say in a research note. The French lender delivered a good set of results, the analysts say. An increase to its payout ratio to half of net profit from 2024 onward isn't totally surprising, RBC says. Moreover, Societe Generale is targeting a return on tangible equity--a key profitability metric--of more than 8% in 2025 and reiterated its guidance of a return of 9%-10% next year. This should give confidence on its outlook, given that consensus expectations are close to the low end of its 2025 guidance but remain below Societe Generale's 2026 targets, RBC says. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0452 GMT - Bank Mandiri (Persero)'s earnings growth is likely to stay stable, Maybank Sekuritas Indonesia analysts say in a research report. The Indonesian bank's net interest margin has shown signs of recovery in 4Q by widening 9bps on quarter, and is likely to continue expanding, the analysts say. The bank's healthy credit quality together with its high non-performing loan coverage of over 250% should keep its credit costs stable going forward, the analysts add. Maybank Sekuritas Indonesia maintains a buy rating but lowers the stock's target price to IDR7,675.00 from IDR8,000.00 partly because the bank's 2024 earnings missed the brokerage's estimates. Shares are 7.2% lower at IDR5,125.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0026 GMT - Pinnacle's bull at Wilsons Advisory believes that its opportunity in Australia's managed-accounts market has yet to be fully priced into its stock. Analyst Cameron Halkett tells clients in a note that he has conviction in the medium-term EPS growth potential from managed accounts offered by Pinnacle affiliate Pacific Asset Management. He points to Life Cycle's strong performance since becoming an affiliate in Pinnacle's most recent fiscal half as evidence of the opportunity on offer. It's possible that Pinnacle could deliver more than A$100 million in gross performance fees in its fiscal second half, Halkett adds. Wilsons lifts its target price 9% to A$28.95 and stays overweight on the stock, which is down 1.7% at A$25.60. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2400 GMT - Computershare could beat its full-year EPS guidance amid slower-than-expected interest-rate cuts in the U.S., Morgan Stanley analysts write in a note. They lift their forecasts for so-called management EPS by 3% in fiscal 2025 and by 6% in fiscal 2026, citing U.S. rates and better margin balances. The Australia-listed share-registry provider benefits from higher rates due to interest earned on funds held for dividends. Morgan Stanley now anticipates 10% growth in fiscal 2025 management EPS, which is about 3% ahead of company guidance. MS lifts its target price by 12% to A$31.10 and stays equal-weight on the stock, which is up 1.1% at A$35.17. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2328 GMT - Bell Potter junks its sell call on Platinum Asset Management, but says the trend for revenue and costs continues to look difficult. Platinum ended talks with Regal Partners over a takeover worth about A$0.90/share late last year. Its stock is currently below A$0.69. That ramps up pressure on Platinum's board to deliver more value than the approach it turned down, analyst Marcus Barnard says. "With revenue still falling, new growth initiatives yet to deliver and the shares trading well below Regal's offer, the company has a lot to prove," Bell Potter says. It moves to hold, from sell. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2221 GMT - Golub Capital's credit and earnings performance will continue to outperform the industry over the next 12 to 24 months, Raymond James analysts say in a research note. The direct lender and private credit asset manager is now above target leverage, faster than Raymond James projected, and producing an attractive return on equity for a lower-than-average risk portfolio, the analysts say. Still, that is priced into shares at current levels, the analysts add. Shares closed down 2.3% to $15.31. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

2217 GMT - Ares Management expects that some of the policies of the new Trump administration will foster dealmaking, giving a further boost to what the private-credit fund manager sees as an already favorable market for investments, Ares CEO Michael Arougheti says during an earnings call. "We expect transaction activity to expand year-over-year," he says. "The new administration could provide some tailwinds for deployment, especially if an improved regulatory and antitrust environment enables more business growth and [mergers and acquisitions] activity in the market." Ares ended December with $133 billion in dry powder, or capital available to spend, a record for the firm. (luis.garcia@wsj.com; @lhvgarcia)

1900 GMT - Fed governor Michelle Bowman notes that Treasury-market liquidity has been a steady source of concern for years, and suggests that regulatory reforms for banks could ease the problem. Speaking at a banking conference in Topeka, Kan., Bowman notes that when bank balance sheets expand, regulatory leverage ratios can become a binding constraint, even if the bank is investing in safe assets. "When constrained in this way, bank-affiliated primary dealers may pull back on market intermediation activities," Bowman says. "Where we can take proactive regulatory measures to ensure that primary dealers have adequate balance sheet capacity to intermediate Treasury markets, we should do so." She suggests amending the leverage ratio and G-SIB surcharge regulations for big U.S. banks could help. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1858 GMT - Investors grew worried about inflation during the trade war flare-up, but the U.S. housing market is likely to counter price pressures elsewhere, Angel Oak's Clayton Triick says. He expects new-home supply to increase, cooling prices and helping keep the Fed on track to cut rates. Housing market dynamics also make investing in mortgages an attractive option for fixed-income investors. Triick is particularly bullish on higher-yielding mortgages not backed by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. "You are getting elevated yields and the fundamentals of housing are still really strong," he says. A drop on house prices could increase defaults, but Triick says it is unlikely to happen. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0904 GMT - Orsted's 2025 Ebitda guidance of 25 billion to 28 billion Danish kroner is in line with consensus at 27.1 billion kroner, RBC Capital Markets analysts Alexander Wheeler and Ziyad Jasimuddin write. Gross investment guidance of 50 billion to 54 billion is also in line with the 52.5 billion expected by RBC. The company yesterday announced it was dropping its 2030 EBITDA and installed capacity targets, while reducing its gross investments to 2030 by around 25%. "We think that the decision to reduce the investment targets will be more of a focus on the earnings call as the market looks to Orsted's balance sheet and what impact this may have." Shares rise 5.5% to 296.30 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0549 GMT - Global Power Synergy's earnings may be impacted by a potential electricity tariff cut in Thailand, CGS International's Amornrat Cheevavichawalkul says in a research report, as the brokerage lowers its target price to THB32.50 from THB43.50. The Energy Regulatory Commission of Thailand has proposed a reduction in electricity tariff to THB3.98/unit, versus the current THB4.15/unit, by removing the adder tariff and feed-in-tariff from the electricity cost structure, the analyst notes. The brokerage estimates that every THB1/unit reduction in the electricity tariff should lead to a 12.8% decline in the Thai power company's 2025 EPS. The brokerage maintains its hold rating as concerns over potentially lower tariffs should mostly be priced in. Shares are 5.9% lower at THB28.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0512 GMT - The scenario for Brent crude oil at $60-$65 per barrel through 2H just got stronger, Citi Research's Francesco Martoccia says in a research report. An increase in non-OPEC+ oil supply remains poised to outpace weak global demand growth from 2Q through 4Q, with the oil market shifting into surplus, the energy strategist says. "We remain strongly of the view that President Trump could ultimately prove to be a bearish influence on the oil market," Martoccia says. He notes that Trump has consistently highlighted lower energy prices as the central solution to issues such as U.S. inflation. Front-month Brent crude oil futures are 0.1% higher at $74.69/bbl; front-month WTI crude oil futures are 0.2% higher at $71.18/bbl. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0413 GMT - Thailand's utilities sector is likely facing major headwinds, Thanachart Securities' Nuttapop Prasitsuksant says in a report. The brokerage downgrades the sector to underweight from overweight. Headwinds include expiring favorable legacy contracts, slow bidding for new projects, and escalating policy risks around tariff rate reductions, the analyst says. The brokerage's new top pick is Gulf Energy Development, as factors like its strong growth outlook seem secured by in-the-pipeline projects with upside from more M&A. Thanachart Securities raises the stock's target to THB65.00 from THB54.00 and maintains a buy rating. Shares are 0.4% higher at THB57.75. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0250 GMT - Keppel Infrastructure Trust's acquisition of a 50% equity interest in Marina East Water looks positive, OCBC Investment Research's Ada Lim says in a note. The bank maintains the trust's buy rating. The acquisition entitles the trust to full economic benefit from MEW, which owns Keppel Marina East Desalination Plant. The acquisition is expected to be accretive to unitholders, as the plant is a high-quality asset that will generate long-term stable cash flows, Lim says. The nature of this asset is also aligned with the trust's portfolio and deepens exposure to water treatment in Singapore, she adds. However, OCBC trims the trust's fair value estimate to S$0.500 from S$0.510 to reflect factors including a higher risk-free rate. Units are unchanged at S$0.450. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2312 GMT - Comet Ridge's signal that a final investment decision on its Mahalo natural-gas project in Australia won't happen this year contributes to Bell Potter cutting its price target by 19% to A$0.21/share. Still, analyst Stuart Howe remains bullish about Comet Ridge, highlighting that Mahalo is one of the few development-ready projects capable of delivering near-term gas supply to eastern Australia. Comet Ridge expects preliminary design and engineering work at Mahalo to continue through this year. Only when this is completed will it make a final investment decision, or FID, with partner Santos. "We expect Comet Ridge's majority equity positions in the Mahalo projects and a strong gas market outlook to provide support for development funding across debt, equity and gas prepayments," Bell Potter says. "However, we also acknowledge risks to FID timing." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2244 GMT - Beach Energy's interim dividend falls short of market expectations, with Barrenjoey noting the payout doesn't align with the company's distribution policy. Beach declared an interim dividend of 3 Australian cents a share. While that was up 50% on last year it fell short of the 4 cents anticipated by the market. Analyst Dale Koenders points out that Beach reported free cash flow of A$431 million in 1H. "But this free cash flow then also implies a payout ratio of only 16%, below policy of 40-50%," Koenders says in his initial response to the result. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1940 GMT - Oil futures fall as the EIA reports a bigger-then-expected 8.7 million barrel increase in U.S. crude inventories. "Tariff developments have buffeted the entire energy complex with potential additional restraints on Iranian crude exports adding to geopolitical uncertainties," Ritterbusch says in a note. "But with the tariffs on Canada and Mexico likely pushed to the sidelines for a couple of weeks, the oil market will be able to zero in more clearly on underlying fundamentals." U.S. gasoline stocks extended their buildup to 12 weeks, while distillate stocks fell by a hefty 5.5 million barrels. "We continue to view gasoline as the weak leg in the complex," Ritterbusch adds. WTI settles down 2.3% at $71.03 a barrel, and Brent falls 2.1% to $74.61. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1845 GMT - A third large weekly inventory draw leaves U.S. distillate stocks 12% below their five-year average as a colder-than-expected January drove extra demand for heating fuel. The inventory decline "is not too surprising given where refineries are operating, still below 85% of operable capacity, but that is something that creates near-term price risk for diesel," says Matt Muenster, chief economist at transport technology firm Breakthrough. Monday's pause in threatened tariffs on Canadian oil took some pressure off prices, and although the cost would likely be shared among Canadian oil producers, U.S. refiners and consumers, "if they had stayed, that ultimately was going to get passed to transportation pretty quickly," he adds. Also offsetting the price impact of lower diesel stocks is the seasonal build in crude oil inventories, Muenster says. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1414 GMT - Equinor cuts capital expenditure as expected, but shareholder distributions are disappointing, Jefferies analysts Giacomo Romeo and Kai Ye Loh write. Updated guidance reflects consensus expectations in terms of the capex cut, due to lower renewables spending. However, 2025 distributions are lower than expected and the outlook for 2026 distributions remains unclear, they say. The 2025 dividend and buyback of $9 billion is below consensus at $10 billion, while Equinor has scrapped its 2026 buyback guidance. Quarterly earnings beat consensus on net income but are relatively inline on the operating line and on cash flow from operations. Shares trade 3.5% lower at 268.20 Norwegian kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1354 GMT - Equinor is moderating its energy transition plans, which should in theory mean more robust cash flows over time, RBC Capital Markets analysts Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani write. The Norwegian energy major's capital allocation plans assume a slowdown in organic offshore wind plans and a slowdown in onshore renewables, they say. Some of its low carbon solutions projects also appear to be slowing, they say. The results look like a slight beat to consensus at the net income level, but guidance for a $5 billion buyback in 2025 is slightly below the $6 billion expected. Total shareholder distributions of $9 billion marks a step down versus 2024 levels of $14.6 billion. "We think there were some lofty expectations into the event around potential distributions." Shares trade 3.2% lower at 268.85 Norwegian kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1247 GMT - Oil futures are losing ground after an industry report of a large build in U.S. crude stocks, while the prospect of a trade war fuels concerns about demand. "All of this tariff talk and general confusion that it's generating is bearish demand as companies can't expand if they don't know what their costs are or what they can sell their goods for," Scott Shelton of TP ICAP says in a note. The API reported a 5 million barrel increase in U.S. stocks for last week, analysts note. EIA data inventory are due later this morning, with a Wall Street Journal survey predicting a 1.3 million barrel build. WTI is off 1% at $71.97 a barrel and Brent is down 1.1% at $75.38 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0906 GMT - Anglo American reported a decent fourth-quarter production update, Barclays analysts write. Its key iron ore, copper and platinum group metals divisions were broadly ahead of consensus. However, sales volumes were mixed while realized prices were weaker than expected, they write. Anglo's medium-term copper guidance is unchanged but new 2027 guidance of 760,000 to 820,000 tons is lower than expected, they write. Shares trade up 4.4% at 2435.50 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0405 GMT - Posco Holdings is set to post weaker-than-expected 2025 earnings from its steel and battery-material businesses, Nomura analyst Cindy Park writes in a note. Park cuts her net-profit forecast for the South Korean steelmaker by 48% this year. She sees a lukewarm business outlook for Posco's steel and battery-material businesses in 2025 with steel demand expected to rise just 1.2% globally and to fall 1.0% in China. It may take time before Posco's asset restructuring makes a meaningful earnings contribution, she adds. Nomura downgrades its rating on the stock to neutral from buy and cuts its target price by 46% to KRW270,000. Shares are 0.6% lower at KRW240,500. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0245 GMT - Palm oil prices rise in early Asian trading, tracking the gains in soybean oil on the Chicago Board of Trade, says David Ng, a trader at Kuala Lumpur-based Iceberg X. Expectations of weaker palm oil output further support CPO prices, he adds. Malaysia's January palm oil output is expected to have declined 10% from the previous month to 1.34 million tons, a Bloomberg survey shows. Ng pegs CPO support at 4,300 ringgit/ton and resistance at 4,450 ringgit/ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for April delivery is 9 ringgit higher at 4,343 ringgit/ton. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2239 GMT - Zircon prices are likely to remain resilient, benefiting Australia's Iluka Resources, says Macquarie. It expects the zircon price to stay above US$1,730/ton in the medium term and then rise in 2029 when the market tips into a deficit. "We also highlight that our supply forecast does not include production rationalization by major producers, which happened during the last zircon price trough in 2016," Macquarie says. Demand trends look positive. The continued push by the Chinese government to ensure property developments are completed supports demand for ceramics, which represents some 50% of zircon consumption, Macquarie says. It keeps an outperform call on Iluka. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2115 GMT - Despite a tough farm economy in 2024, Corteva expects improvements this year. "We acknowledge the fluid macro environment but still see ag market fundamentals improving - specifically, we see continued record demand for core crops and recovering grain prices and farm margins," CEO Chuck Magro said in a statement. Shares of the agriculture company ticked down 6% during post-market trading after it said sales rose 7% to about $4 billion in its latest quarter, slightly below analyst expectations for the period. Corteva, one of the largest sellers of crop seeds and chemicals in the world, and other agriculture companies faced a tough agriculture market this past year because of declining grain prices and rising costs. Corteva said sales volumes for its pesticides and seeds both were up double digits for the quarter that ended Dec. 31 compared with the prior year as prices for its products ticked down. (patrick.thomas@wsj.com)

1831 GMT - Front-month gold futures settle again at a new record, with that contract closing up 0.6% to $2,871.60 a troy ounce. It's the third consecutive session that gold climbed, and the sixth positive close in the past seven sessions. Amid confusion surrounding U.S. tariffs and retaliation, physical gold demand is strong, says Phil Flynn of Price Futures Group. "We're starting to see huge demand for physical gold and reports that the Comex exchange is having to secure more supplies to satisfy massive gold deliveries," says Flynn in a note. SPDR Gold shares are also higher in late trading, with shares up 0.8%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1512 GMT - Home Depot and Lowe's might report a better 4Q than Wall Street expects given recent results from the home-improvement chains' suppliers, Stanley Black & Decker, Sherwin-Williams and Scotts Miracle-Gro, Davidson analyst Michael Baker says in a research note. Stanley Black & Decker reports 2% organic growth in tools and outdoor products, an improvement from the 4% decline last quarter and the best increase since 2Q of 2021, the analyst says. Sherwin-Williams' consumer brands business, which is Lowe's main paint supplier, was flat in North America instead of down in the high-single digit percentage range like the three prior quarters, he says. And Scotts Miracle-Gro showed an acceleration of its gains from the prior quarter, the analyst says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1428 GMT - U.S. growth came in surprisingly strong in 2024 versus start-of-year projections, and most economists expect the momentum to carry into 2025. But while the consumer economy and the services sector are thriving, industrial companies are complaining of a grimmer backdrop. "Recent economic activity is primarily being led by the strength in service-related sectors," while the physical economy has been weaker, Dow CFO Jeff Tate tells analysts on a recent earnings call. Dow executives several times on the call referred to a "prolonged economic downturn." Eastman Chemical CEO Mark Costa gave a similar take on the company's recent analyst call. "The macro economy is certainly challenging right now as we all know," he said. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1342 GMT - U.S. farmers may see a significant price hike for fertilizer if Trump tariffs are implemented, as the materials used for fertilizer are often imported into the U.S, says Farmdoc Daily in a note. Potassium-based fertilizers may take the brunt, as 90% of U.S. consumption of potassium comes from imports. Canada is the leading producer of potash globally, and a tariff could add over $100 per ton for U.S. farmers to the current price tag around $450 a ton, says Farmdoc. Nitrogen fertilizers would also be impacted, although imports account for far less consumption, according to data from the U.S. Geological Survey. Nitrogen fertilizers are the most important for U.S. corn, while U.S. soybeans are often grown using potassium-based products. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0545 GMT - Samsung Heavy Industries is expected to exceed its 2025 guidance for new contract wins, Nomura analyst Eon Hwang writes in a note. Hwang expects new orders for the South Korean shipbuilder to jump 59% to $12 billion this year, beating its own target of $9.8 billion. Samsung Heavy's operating profit is projected to rise 54% in 2025, Hwang says. He is also upbeat on Samsung Heavy bidding for two floating liquefied natural gas projects this year--one off Mozambique and the other in the U.S. Nomura raises its target price for the stock by 14% to KRW16,000 and keeps a buy rating. Shares are 1.8% lower at KRW13,160. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0506 GMT - The magnitude of price cuts in China's electric-vehicle industry this year should be much less than last year, Jefferies analysts write in a note. Since inventories at BYD dealers are much lower and the profitability of new car sales is much better, the analysts reckon BYD is unlikely to kick off an aggressive price war like last year. Among the top Chinese EV makers, Xiaomi, Huawei-backed Harmony Intelligent Mobility Alliance and XPeng are outperformers by new order intakes, they note. The introduction of Tesla's full-self driving features in China this year could serve as a good example for its Chinese rivals on how carmakers can commercialize their advanced assisted driving technologies, they add. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0405 GMT - Toyota Motor's plan to build a new Lexus factory in Shanghai to develop and produce electric vehicles and batteries is a positive for China's auto sector, Daiwa analyst Kelvin Lau writes in a note. The newly formed entity in Shanghai will develop an EV model under the Lexus brand, with production to start from 2027. The initial production capacity will be around 100,000 units annually. The plan reflects Toyota's commitment to the Chinese EV market, and implies opportunities for the carmaker's existing and potential partners, Lau says. Daiwa remains cautiously positive due to limited details regarding the Shanghai factory, but it's an important step for Toyota to achieve its 2035 electrification goal. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0314 GMT - Yangzijiang Shipbuilding's management is expected to guide for higher new order wins this year, says UOB Kay Hian analyst Adrian Loh. The shipbuilder's new Yangzi Hongyuan yard in China will be ready for operation in 2H 2026, he writes in a note. With the new capacity taken into consideration, the brokerage estimates Yangzijiang Shipbuilding's new order win target could be between US$6 billion and US$7 billion. The company has a 2024 order win target of US$4.5 billion. UOB Kay Hian has a buy rating on the stock and a target price of S$3.60. Shares are flat at S$3.07.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

2229 GMT - Ford would be able to manage a few weeks of President Trump's proposed tariffs given the rate and flow of its products, CEO Jim Farley says on a call with analysts. If they persisted, though, they would have a devastating impact, he says. The company already has plans to expand some of its U.S. plants to mitigate higher import costs, but if the proposed tariffs become a long-term reality, Ford would have to make some major strategy shifts, such as building all new plants in the U.S., Farley says. Shares fall 5.1% after hours. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

2214 GMT - Ford CEO Jim Farley says on a call with analysts that President Trump's proposed tariffs could hurt the U.S. auto industry. "There's no question that tariffs at the 25% level from Canada and Mexico, if they're protracted, would have a huge impact on our industry, with billions of dollars of industry profits wiped out and adverse effects on U.S. jobs," he says. Consumers would also face higher prices, he adds. Ford applauds Trump's administration for the agreements with Mexico and Canada earlier this week and says it is closely monitoring the situation in China. Shares fall 5.3%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

2208 GMT - Ford CEO Jim Farley says on a call with analysts that the company believes President Trump and his administration, as well as other congressional leaders, are committed to strengthening the U.S. auto industry. "That is certainly our expectation, and we look forward to working with our leaders to make sure that that becomes a reality, because they understand and appreciate how vital our industry is to jobs, the economy, our national security and the communities across our country," he says. Shares fall 5.3% after the company says it expects profitability to shrink in 2025. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

2234 GMT - Air New Zealand looks a good bet to achieve the upper end of 1H profit guidance following a solid finish to the period, Macquarie says. Revenue per available seat kilometer, a key performance metric for airlines, rose 0.7% in 1H when currency swings and travel credits are excluded. That reversed a fall in RASK through November. Air New Zealand previously signaled expectations for a 1H pretax profit of NZ$120 million-NZ$160 million. "With a strong December set of operating statistics, we expect Air New Zealand to be at the upper end of this range," Macquarie says. "While 2H has challenges, we see stabilization rather than any further deterioration." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1959 GMT - Uber Technologies' latest results gave a lot of details the market has yet to digest, such as another frequency growth deceleration in membership gains, with the 1Q outlook calling for more of the same, Benchmark analysts say in a research note. The ride sharing app's incremental margins also took a 1% step down from where they had been running as it continues to invest in growth initiatives, the analysts say. But when it comes to the autonomous vehicle race, Uber has a real opportunity to showcase their value in Austin and Atlanta this year, with full scaled offerings and demand chain management starting in March, the analysts add. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1952 GMT - Uber Technologies's approach to the autonomous vehicles market isn't convincing investors yet, Melius Research analysts say in a research note. The ride-sharing app provided a little more clarity on its strategy regarding AVs on a long-term basis, but that doesn't seem to be enough to change the conversation and therefore created volatility, the analysts say. "With AV development still in early days, it's hard to tell how Uber ultimately fits in," the analysts say. "There is merit to Uber driving utilization and demand, but as Waymo, Tesla, etc. introduce their own platforms, they will gather data and build knowledge around demand patterns as well," the analysts add. Shares fall 7.6% to $64.46. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1520 GMT - Harley-Davidson CEO Jochen Zeitz says the company's electric motorcycle spinoff "needs to perform" following another disappointing year. LiveWire sold 612 bikes in 2024, far below its expectation of up to 1,500 bikes, and lost $110 million. Its CEO, Karim Donnez, says the company is cutting costs and hopes to succeed in Europe with a new "maxi-scooter." Harley owns nearly 90% of LiveWire, and some analysts say the spinoff's losses are dragging down Harley's financial results. Zeitz says while the slow adoption of EV vehicles has hurt LiveWire, "we'll obviously have to look at some optionalities" if results don't improve. (john.keilman@wsj.com)

1155 GMT - Uber says it expects gross bookings in the current quarter of $42 billion-$43.5 billion, with the top end of that range coming in line with Wall Street's consensus view, according to FactSet. The 1Q outlook assumes a currency headwind to total growth of about 5.5 percentage points, the ride-hailing company says. Shares fall 5.3% premarket, despite 4Q results otherwise coming in ahead of Street views. (mark.long@wsj.com)

data.title: Castrol India up 18% in 3 days on profit jump, ratings upgrades
data.description:
** Shares of Castrol India jump 7% to seven-week high of 207.90 rupees

** Stock up 18% in 3 days after co posts 12% increase in Q3 net profit on Monday on the back of steady demand

** Kotak Institutional Equities double upgraded CAST to "add" from "sell" after the results

** Says stock "no longer expensive" after correction from August peak

** IDBI Capital upgraded stock to "buy" from "hold", highlighting company's aim to outpace industry growth, attractive valuations

** CAST currently down 27% from record high of 284 rupees hit on August 27

** Analysts average rating on stock "buy"; median PT 235 rupees - LSEG data

data.title: Market Chatter: ByteDance Said to Hold Up Negotiations for TikTok US Sale as China Seen to Pull Out
data.description:
ByteDance is said to stall negotiations for the sale of TikTok's US operations as it awaits the green light from the Chinese government, The Washington Post reported Wednesday, citing three people familiar with the talks.

The development comes even as the allies of US President Donald Trump are scrambling to sell the app to a US bidder, the report said, citing the sources.

Beijing is likely to let TikTok's US operations fade away than approve the sale as it looks to wait for a "grand deal" that may include more favorable trade and tech approaches, the report said, citing sources.

The tactic could work against Trump's efforts to "save TikTok" by suspending the implementation of a law that would force the sale of the app to an American bidder or shut down US operations, The Washington Post said.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Korea Zinc Swings to Loss in Q4 2024 Despite Higher Sales
data.description:
Korea Zinc swung to a net loss attributable to shareholders of the parent of 228.6 billion won in the fourth quarter of 2024, from an attributable net income of 189.8 billion won a year earlier.

Sales, however, jumped 42.6% year over year to 3.443 trillion won from 2.415 trillion won, according to a Wednesday Korea Exchange filing.

Shares of Korea Zinc fell nearly 1% in recent trade on Thursday.

data.title: Iceland Trade Deficit Narrows Sharply
data.description:
Iceland’s trade deficit narrowed sharply to ISK 7.6 billion in January 2025 from ISK 19.7 billion in the corresponding month of the previous year.

Exports soared 19% year-on-year to ISK 93.9 billion, primarily attributed to higher sales of manufacturing products (35%), farmed fish (22%), and agricultural products (12%).

Meanwhile, imports rose at a much softer pace of 3% to ISK 101.5 billion, supported by increased purchases of industrial supplies n.e.s (27%) and transport equipment (14%).

However, purchases declined for fuels and lubricants (-25%), consumer goods n.e.s (-10%), and food and beverages (-9%).

data.title: Nestcon Gets More Time to Complete Private Placement of Shares
data.description:
Nestcon secured an extension of time up to July 30 to complete the private placement, according to a Wednesday Malaysian bourse filing.

According to earlier filings, the property developer had proposed to issue up to 10% of its total issued shares to private investors.
